question_title: Will NASA's X-59 achieve its first supersonic flight (exceeding Mach 1.0) before June 14, 2026?
summary: NASA's X-59 is very well-positioned for its first supersonic flight within the resolution window. The aircraft has reached Mach 0.95 at the target altitude, NASA made a specific "early June" announcement on May 28, 2026, 15 successful flights demonstrate high operational readiness, and no issues have been reported. The main risk is the tight 13-day window — any slip of ~2 weeks in the schedule pushes past resolution. Decomposition: P(attempted by Jun 13) ≈ 87% × P(successful | attempted) ≈ 93% × P(reported | successful) ≈ 99% ≈ 80%, adjusted slightly upward to 82% accounting for possible retry within the window.

factors.description: NASA officially announced 'early June' supersonic flight on May 28, 2026 - very specific, recent commitment with high credibility
Aircraft at Mach 0.95 at target altitude (43,000 ft) - only ~5% speed increment to Mach 1.0+, minimal technical gap remaining
15 successful flights in ~3 months; Block 1 formally completed, review passed, new flight block approved
No reports of delays, grounding, or technical problems as of June 1 - all coverage is positive and readiness-focused
Only 13-day resolution window (June 1-13) - even a 1-2 week slip from 'early June' target causes NO resolution
Weather, unexpected transonic/supersonic technical issues, or Edwards AFB range availability could delay by days
Edwards AFB June weather is generally excellent (dry, clear desert) - favorable for flight testing

reasoning:
**Forecast: 82% probability that NASA's X-59 achieves its first supersonic flight before June 14, 2026.**

**Key reasoning:**

- **Strong YES signals:** NASA's May 28 announcement specifically targeting "early June," aircraft already at Mach 0.95 (only 5% speed increment needed), 15 successful flights showing high operational tempo, Block 1 complete with formal review passed, and no reported issues.

- **Main NO risk:** The 13-day resolution window is tight. "Early June" is aspirational language, and even a brief technical issue or weather delay at Edwards AFB could push the attempt past June 13. The transonic-to-supersonic regime can reveal unexpected aerodynamic behavior despite successful subsonic testing.

- **Decomposition:** P(attempted by Jun 13) ~87% × P(successful) ~93% × P(reported) ~99% ≈ 82%
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will NASA's X-59 achieve its first supersonic flight (exceeding Mach 1.0) before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43741)
[NASA’s X-59 Prepares for First Supersonic Flight - NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/centers-and-facilities/armstrong/nasas-x-59-prepares-for-first-supersonic-flight/)

[NASA tweaks X-59 software, flight control systems ahead of first supersonic attempt in ‘early June’](https://aerospaceamerica.aiaa.org/nasa-tweaks-x-59-software-flight-control-systems-ahead-of-first-supersonic-attempt-in-early-june/)
[NASA’s X-59 May Be Days Away From Supersonic Flight](https://www.flyingmag.com/nasa-x59-weeks-away-first-supersonic-flight/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between May 31, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on June 13, 2026, NASA's X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft completes a flight in which it exceeds Mach 1.0, as reported by NASA's Quesst blog (https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/quesst/), NASA Armstrong Flight Research Center (https://www.nasa.gov/armstrong/), or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). Otherwise, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: A "supersonic flight" means the X-59 aircraft itself achieved an airspeed greater than Mach 1.0 during powered flight; ground tests, simulator runs, or chase aircraft going supersonic do not count. If NASA reports the flight occurred but the exact Mach number is not yet published by June 14, 2026, a NASA statement that the X-59 "broke the sound barrier" or "went supersonic" is sufficient for YES.

factors.description: NASA officially announced 'early June' supersonic flight on May 28, 2026 - very specific, near-term commitment from the agency
Aircraft at Mach 0.95 at target altitude - technically only 5% speed increment needed, minimal technical gap
15 flights in ~3 months shows high operational tempo; Block 1 complete, formal review passed, new block approved
No reports of delays, grounding, or technical problems as of June 1 - entirely positive news coverage
Only 13-day resolution window (June 1-13) - even a 1-2 week slip from 'early June' target would cause NO resolution
Weather, unexpected transonic/supersonic technical issues, or range availability could delay by days
Edwards AFB June weather is generally excellent (dry, clear desert) - favorable for flight testing

input.anchor: Experimental flight test programs meeting announced near-term schedule targets: ~60-65%. Based on general flight test program schedule adherence, adjusted for the fact that this is an incremental envelope expansion (not first flight), which has higher on-time rates.
input.assessment:
Strong evidence points toward YES: NASA made a very specific, recent (May 28) announcement targeting early June, the aircraft is extremely close technically (Mach 0.95 → 1.0+), operational tempo is high (15 flights in 3 months), and there are no reported issues. The main risk is the tight 13-day window - any slip of ~2 weeks pushes past resolution. I estimate ~82% probability, reflecting genuine confidence tempered by the real possibility of short delays in experimental flight testing.

Decomposition: P(attempted by Jun 13) ≈ 87% × P(successful | attempted) ≈ 93% × P(reported | successful) ≈ 99% ≈ 80%. Adding back probability of retry within window if first attempt has issues brings this to ~82%.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor at 65% updated to 82% by strong positive evidence. Not going higher because: (1) 13-day window is genuinely tight for experimental aircraft, (2) transonic/supersonic regime can reveal unexpected issues even when subsonic testing went perfectly, (3) 'early June' could mean the attempt is planned for June 8-10, leaving very little margin. Status quo check: if nothing happens, this resolves NO - correct to keep probability below 90%.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Exact date NASA plans for the supersonic attempt within 'early June'. 2. Whether transonic buffet, flutter, or other issues emerge between Mach 0.95 and 1.0. 3. Whether weather at Edwards causes multi-day delays.
input.tool_audit: research() provided good current data on second query. web_search and fetch_url were the most valuable - confirmed NASA announcement details and found no reports of delays. All tools functioned well.
input.update_triggers: NASA blog announcing supersonic flight → 99%. Report of technical grounding → 30-40%. Multiple days pass with no flight activity reported → gradual decline.
input.process_reflection: Good workflow - the question is straightforward with clear resolution criteria. The main analytical work is estimating schedule adherence probability for a tight window. The decomposition into P(attempted) × P(successful) × P(reported) was helpful.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Factors checked against trace:**

1. **NASA "early June" announcement (May 28, 2026)** — Confirmed. The trace includes a direct fetch of the NASA Armstrong article (https://www.nasa.gov/centers-and-facilities/armstrong/nasas-x-59-prepares-for-first-supersonic-flight/) which states "early June" for the first supersonic flight. Multiple corroborating sources (Flying Magazine, Orbital Today, ScienceDaily, Aerospace America) all confirm this. ✅

2. **Aircraft at Mach 0.95 at target altitude** — Confirmed. NASA article fetch result states "reached up to Mach 0.95 (627 mph) and 43,000 feet." ✅

3. **15 flights in ~3 months; Block 1 complete, review passed** — Confirmed. Research result states "14 additional flights" since March 2026 (15 total). Aerospace America article confirms Block One flights "are now concluded, NASA confirmed by email." ✅

4. **No reports of delays/grounding** — Confirmed. The agent ran a targeted web_search for `"X-59" supersonic flight June 2026 delay OR postponed OR grounded` and found no X-59 delays. The "grounded" result was about SpaceX Starship, not X-59. Note: the Aerospace America article mentions ICAWS alarm and software tweaks, which the agent appropriately addresses in the counterargument rather than ignoring. ✅

5. **13-day window** — Correctly derived from resolution criteria ("between May 31, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on June 13, 2026"). ✅

6-7. **Weather/technical risks and Edwards AFB weather** — Inferential factors, not factual claims requiring trace support. Reasonable. ✅

**No double-counting detected.** Factors 1-4 draw from the same NASA article but capture distinct evidence dimensions (timeline commitment, technical readiness, operational tempo, absence of problems). Logit magnitudes are moderate and appropriate.

**No wrong-direction factors.** All positive factors have positive logits; negative factors have negative logits.

**Resolution criteria properly engaged.** The agent fetched the Metaculus question, correctly identified the May 31–June 13 window, checked the specified NASA sources, and factors directly address the resolution criteria terms.

**Counterargument is substantive.** It identifies "early June" as aspirational vs committed, discusses transonic regime unpredictability, notes the ICAWS alarm issue as a yellow flag, cites NASA's culture of caution, and correctly estimates only 2-3 flight attempts possible in the window. This is a genuine adversarial case, not a token gesture.

**Probability assessment: 82% is well-calibrated.** The decomposition (P(attempted) ~87% × P(success|attempted) ~93% × P(reported) ~99% ≈ 80%, adjusted to 82%) is sound. The anchor departure from 60-65% to 82% is justified by the very recent NASA announcement (Factor 1, logit=2.0) and the aircraft being at Mach 0.95 — these are strong, verified pieces of evidence that distinguish this from a generic "flight test meeting schedule" base rate. My independent estimate would be in the 78-85% range, consistent with the agent's 82%.

**Self-confidence (0.60) is appropriate** given genuine uncertainty about whether "early June" extends through June 13.

**Tool audit is accurate.** Agent's claims about tool performance match the ground truth metrics (0 errors across all tools).

**No worldview contradictions.** No prior X-59 forecasts or research entries conflict with the current assessment.

No errors found warranting a warn or fail.
